home.aboutP1: On August 15, 1947, India gained its independence from British rule after a long and hard-fought struggle. This day marks the birth of a free and democratic nation. We celebrate the courage, sacrifice, and resilience of our freedom fighters who laid down their lives for our future.
home.aboutP2: Today, we remember our history and look forward to a brighter future, upholding the values of unity in diversity.

history.pageSubtitle: A journey through centuries of courage, sacrifice, and the unwavering spirit of freedom
history.heroOverlayTitle: The Story of a Nation's Awakening
history.heroOverlaySubtitle: From the arrival of European traders to the dawn of freedom — the greatest struggle in human history.
history.era1Card1Text: The British East India Company arrived in India in 1608 as traders seeking spices, textiles, and riches. They established their first factory in Surat in 1613. Through political manipulation, military conquests, and exploitation of rivalries between Indian rulers, the Company gradually expanded its control across the subcontinent.
history.era1Card2Text: Robert Clive's decisive victory over Nawab Siraj-ud-Daulah at the Battle of Plassey in 1757 marked the beginning of British political control in India. This was achieved largely through treachery and bribery, setting the stage for nearly two centuries of colonial exploitation.
history.era1Card3Text: The British systematically drained India's wealth. India's share of world GDP fell from 24.4% in 1700 to just 4.2% by 1950. Indian artisans and weavers were deliberately ruined to create markets for British manufactured goods. Devastating famines killed millions while grain was exported to Britain.
history.era2Text1: The Indian Rebellion of 1857, also known as the Sepoy Mutiny or the First War of Indian Independence, was a major uprising against the British East India Company's rule. Triggered by the introduction of new Enfield rifles with cartridges rumored to be greased with cow and pig fat, the revolt quickly spread from Meerut across northern and central India.
history.era2Text2: Brave leaders like Mangal Pandey, Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi, Tantia Tope, Bahadur Shah Zafar, and Nana Sahib led the resistance. Though the rebellion was ultimately suppressed, it shook the foundations of British rule and led to the dissolution of the East India Company. The British Crown took direct control of India, beginning the era of the British Raj.
history.era2Quote: "I shall not surrender my Jhansi." — Rani Lakshmibai
history.era3Card1Text: Founded in 1885 by A.O. Hume, the Indian National Congress became the principal voice of the independence movement. Early leaders like Dadabhai Naoroji, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, and Surendranath Banerjee advocated for greater Indian representation in governance through petitions and dialogue.
history.era3Card2Text: Triggered by the partition of Bengal by Lord Curzon, the Swadeshi Movement was a powerful economic strategy to boycott British goods and promote Indian-made products. The spinning wheel (Charkha) became a symbol of self-reliance, and Indian textile mills flourished as people pledged to wear only Khadi.
history.era3Card3Text: Leaders like Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Lala Lajpat Rai, and Bipin Chandra Pal (the Lal-Bal-Pal trio) believed in Swaraj through more assertive means. Tilak's famous declaration — "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it!" — electrified the nation.
history.era3Card4Text: On 13 April 1919, General Dyer ordered troops to fire on thousands of unarmed civilians gathered at Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar. Hundreds were killed and over 1,200 wounded. This horrific act of brutality shattered any remaining faith in British justice and transformed the freedom movement.
history.era4Text: Mahatma Gandhi's return to India in 1915 transformed the independence movement from an elite affair into a mass people's struggle. His philosophy of Satyagraha (truth-force) and Ahimsa (non-violence) gave ordinary Indians powerful weapons against colonial oppression.
history.era4Quote: "First they ignore you, then they laugh at you, then they fight you, then you win." — Mahatma Gandhi
history.era4Item1Text: Indians boycotted British goods, courts, schools, and government services. Millions returned British titles and honors. Foreign cloth bonfires spread across India.
history.era4Item2Text: Gandhi led 78 volunteers on a 388 km march from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi beach to break the British salt monopoly — one of the most iconic acts of civil disobedience in history.
history.era4Item3Text: With his thundering call of "Do or Die", Gandhi launched the final mass movement demanding an immediate end to British rule. Despite brutal repression and mass arrests, it demonstrated India's absolute resolve for freedom.
history.era5Card1Text: Young revolutionaries who believed that armed resistance was necessary to overthrow British tyranny. Bhagat Singh threw bombs in the Central Legislative Assembly not to kill, but to "make the deaf hear." Their execution at ages 23 inspired millions to join the freedom struggle.
history.era5Card1Quote: "They may kill me, but they cannot kill my ideas." — Bhagat Singh
history.era5Card2Text: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose formed the Indian National Army (Azad Hind Fauj) with the support of Japan during World War II. His call of "Tum mujhe khoon do, main tumhe azaadi dunga!" (Give me blood and I will give you freedom!) rallied thousands of Indian soldiers to fight for liberation.
history.era5Card2Quote: "It is blood alone that can pay the price of freedom." — Subhas Chandra Bose
history.era6Text1: After World War II weakened the British Empire, and with the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny of 1946 proving that the military could no longer be relied upon, the British finally agreed to transfer power. However, independence came at the devastating cost of Partition — the division of British India into India and Pakistan.
history.era6Text2: The partition triggered one of the largest mass migrations in human history, with an estimated 15 million people displaced and up to 2 million killed in communal violence. Families were torn apart, and communities that had coexisted for centuries were divided by hastily drawn borders.
history.era6Text3: Despite the immense tragedy, on the midnight of 14–15 August 1947, India awoke to freedom. Jawaharlal Nehru's immortal "Tryst with Destiny" speech echoed across the nation:
history.era6Quote: "At the stroke of the midnight hour, when the world sleeps, India will awake to life and freedom. A moment comes, which comes but rarely in history, when we step out from the old to the new, when an age ends, and when the soul of a nation, long suppressed, finds utterance." — Jawaharlal Nehru

freedomFighters.pageSubtitle: Remembering the heroes who gave us our tomorrow

nationalSymbols.anthemAboutText: The National Anthem of India "Jana Gana Mana", composed originally in Bengali by Rabindranath Tagore, was adopted in its Hindi version by the Constituent Assembly as the National Anthem of India on 24 January 1950. It was first sung on 27 December 1911 at the Calcutta Session of the Indian National Congress.
nationalSymbols.songAboutText1: The song "Vande Mataram", composed in Sanskrit by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, was a source of inspiration to the people in their struggle for freedom. It has an equal status with Jana Gana Mana. The first political occasion when it was sung was the 1896 session of the Indian National Congress.
nationalSymbols.songAboutText2: It was originally part of Bankim Chandra's novel "Anandamath" published in 1882. The song played a vital role in the Indian independence movement and was first sung in a political context at the 1896 session of the Indian National Congress.
nationalSymbols.songAboutText3: On 24 January 1950, President Dr. Rajendra Prasad stated in the Constituent Assembly that "the song Vande Mataram, which has played a historic part in the struggle for Indian freedom, shall be honoured equally with Jana Gana Mana and shall have equal status with it."
nationalSymbols.otherSymbolsSubtitle: Symbols that represent India's rich heritage, culture, and natural diversity
nationalSymbols.flagDesc: The National Flag of India is a horizontal tricolour of deep saffron (courage), white (truth & peace) with the Ashoka Chakra (wheel of law with 24 spokes) in navy blue at the centre, and India green (fertility & prosperity) at the bottom. Adopted on 22 July 1947, the flag was designed by Pingali Venkayya.
nationalSymbols.emblemDesc: The State Emblem of India is an adaptation of the Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath. It features four Asiatic lions standing back to back, symbolising power, courage, pride, and confidence. The motto "Satyameva Jayate" (Truth Alone Triumphs) is inscribed below. Adopted on 26 January 1950.
nationalSymbols.animalDesc: The magnificent Royal Bengal Tiger is India's national animal, symbolising grace, strength, agility, and power. India is home to about 70% of the world's tiger population. Project Tiger was launched in 1973 to protect this majestic animal from extinction.
nationalSymbols.birdDesc: The Indian Peacock, with its resplendent plumage of iridescent blue and green feathers, was declared the national bird of India in 1963. It symbolises grace, joy, beauty, and pride. The peacock is deeply woven into Indian mythology, art, and culture.
nationalSymbols.flowerDesc: The Lotus is the national flower of India, symbolising divinity, fertility, wealth, knowledge, and purity. It holds a sacred place in Indian culture and mythology. The lotus blooms beautifully even in muddy waters, representing triumph of the spirit over adversity.
nationalSymbols.fruitDesc: The Mango, known as the "King of Fruits", has been cultivated in India for over 5,000 years. It symbolises prosperity and fertility. India is the world's largest producer of mangoes, with over 100 varieties grown across the country, including the famous Alphonso, Dasheri, and Langra.
nationalSymbols.treeDesc: The Indian Banyan tree represents eternal life due to its seemingly ever-expanding branches. It is often the focal point of village life ("Panchayat tree"). The Great Banyan in Kolkata's Botanical Garden is over 250 years old and covers approximately 14,500 sq. metres.
nationalSymbols.riverDesc: The Ganga is the longest river of India, flowing 2,525 km from the Gangotri glacier in the Himalayas to the Bay of Bengal. Revered as "Ganga Maa" (Mother Ganga), it is the lifeline for millions of people, supporting agriculture, commerce, and spiritual practices across the Indo-Gangetic plain.
nationalSymbols.aquaticDesc: The Gangetic River Dolphin was declared the national aquatic animal of India in 2009. This nearly blind freshwater dolphin navigates using ultrasonic echolocation. It is an indicator of the health of the river ecosystem and is classified as endangered, with fewer than 2,000 remaining.
nationalSymbols.heritageDesc: The Indian Elephant was declared the National Heritage Animal in 2010 by the Government of India. Elephants have been an integral part of Indian culture, religion, and history for millennia. India is home to about 60% of the Asian elephant population, primarily in the Western Ghats and northeastern states.
nationalSymbols.reptileDesc: The King Cobra, the world's longest venomous snake, is the national reptile of India. It can grow up to 18 feet long and is revered in Indian mythology, often depicted with Lord Shiva. Found in the rainforests and plains of India, it holds a significant place in Indian folklore and culture.
nationalSymbols.currencyDesc: The Indian Rupee (₹) is the official currency of India, issued by the Reserve Bank of India. The symbol ₹ was adopted on 15 July 2010, designed by D. Udaya Kumar. It is derived from the Devanagari consonant "र" (Ra) and the Latin letter "R", representing India's international identity.

about.pageSubtitle: A young learner with a passion for knowledge, creativity, and positive growth.
about.whoP1: Rishabh Singh is a student of Class 5 at Pawar Public School. He is an enthusiastic learner with a keen interest in academics, sports, and extracurricular activities.
about.whoP2: He is known for his positive attitude, curiosity, and willingness to learn new things.
about.interests: Reading, Science, Sports, Art, and Technology
about.characterP1: Through his participation in classroom activities and school programs, Rishabh continues to build confidence, discipline, and leadership qualities.
about.characterP2: He aims to continue learning, growing, and contributing positively to his school community.
